coredata.dc:description: The alarming increase in environmental contamination caused by the untreated release of hazardous phenolic compounds such as 3-Aminophenol (3-AP) and Safranin-O (SO) dye has become a serious global issue. Traditional cleanup methods have limitations, so we must decline practical approaches that do not harm the environment. The purpose of this research was to develop a new catalytic system for advanced oxidation processes (AOPs) by combining nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) with nickel oxide (NiO) nanocomposites. Citrus limetta leaves were utilized to create N-CQDs, which were then combined with NiO nanoparticles via green tea extract. A thorough investigation revealed that N-CQDs were successfully added to the NiO matrix, increasing the nanocomposite’s structural stability and photocatalytic properties. Sunlight-based tests showed that N-CQDs@NiO nanocomposites were better at breaking down chemicals than NiO nanoparticles alone, with 99 % efficiency for 3-AP and 98 % efficiency for SO. The higher efficiency was due to N-CQDs’ better ability to adsorb substances, keep particles from sticking together, and separate charges effectively, which made it easier for reactive species like superoxide anions and hydroxyl radicals to form. This work shows that N-CQDs@NiO nanocomposites could clean up the environment and provide a long-term way to break down persistent organic pollutants in wastewater. In the future, researchers will try to improve the process of making these nanocomposites and find ways to use them in real wastewater treatment systems.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The alarming increase in environmental contamination caused by the untreated release of hazardous phenolic compounds such as 3-Aminophenol (3-AP) and Safranin-O (SO) dye has become a serious global issue. Traditional cleanup methods have limitations, so we must decline practical approaches that do not harm the environment. The purpose of this research was to develop a new catalytic system for advanced oxidation processes (AOPs) by combining nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) with nickel oxide (NiO) nanocomposites. Citrus limetta leaves were utilized to create N-CQDs, which were then combined with NiO nanoparticles via green tea extract. A thorough investigation revealed that N-CQDs were successfully added to the NiO matrix, increasing the nanocomposite’s structural stability and photocatalytic properties. Sunlight-based tests showed that N-CQDs@NiO nanocomposites were better at breaking down chemicals than NiO nanoparticles alone, with 99 % efficiency for 3-AP and 98 % efficiency for SO. The higher efficiency was due to N-CQDs’ better ability to adsorb substances, keep particles from sticking together, and separate charges effectively, which made it easier for reactive species like superoxide anions and hydroxyl radicals to form. This work shows that N-CQDs@NiO nanocomposites could clean up the environment and provide a long-term way to break down persistent organic pollutants in wastewater. In the future, researchers will try to improve the process of making these nanocomposites and find ways to use them in real wastewater treatment systems.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The untreated release of hazardous phenolic complexes from various industrial processes has led to an alarming rise in environmental contamination, becoming a major universal problem [1,2]. Among these harmful compounds, 3-Aminophenol (3-AP) has gained much attention due to its widespread use and negative impact on human health and the atmosphere. Acquaintance with this pollutant has been linked to several health problems, including cancer, reproductive damage, and endocrine disruption [3]. 3-AP is a highly hazardous intermediate that is commonly utilized in the manufacture of pharmaceuticals and dyes. Its increased hazard is due to its high solubility (26 g/L) in water and low biodegradability [7]. 3-AP is abundant in the environment, including surface water, soil, and groundwater. The primary mechanisms of exposure to 3-AP include ingestion of contaminated water and food, inhalation, and skin absorption [8]. Thus, developing effective and environmentally friendly methods for degrading and removing this persistent pollutant is crucial [9,10]. In addition to 3-AP, Safranin-O (SO) dye causes significant environmental issues. SO is extensively used in histology and cytology in the pharmaceutical and biochemical industries [11]. It can stain Gram-negative bacteria and detect cartilage, mucin, and mast cell granules [11,12]. Furthermore, SO produces tannins, cotton, fibers, wool, silk leather, and paper [13–18]. It is often used to flavor and color desserts and pastries [19]. However, SO can cause health issues such as eye irritation, burning, dermatitis, eczema, and respiratory allergies [20]. Table 1 lists the different materials used to remove SO and 3-AP. Given the dangers to surface water, wastewater must be treated to remove SO [18] altogether.

Adsorption, biological treatment, and chemical oxidation are three traditional phenolic chemical and dye remediation approaches that have been thoroughly explored. However, these systems usually have drawbacks, including low efficiency for resistant contaminants, high operating costs, and the need to clear this waste. Advanced oxidation processes (AOPs) break down organic pollutants by producing reactive species such as hydroxyl radicals (⋅OH), which can mineralize impurities into CO2 and water [21–26].

Recent research has focused on developing novel, highly efficient catalytic systems that are ecologically friendly for AOPs. Because of their unique properties, nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (n-CQDs) coupled with nickel oxide (NiO) have shown extraordinary potential among the various catalytic materials explored. These qualities include a large surface area, varied porosity, enhanced chemical and thermal stability, and the ability to include a wide range of active species in their structure [27–29]. The N-CQDs@NiO nanocomposite has been shown to enhance Photocatalytic activity and its selectivity for a wide range of oxidation processes by improving the generation of reactive species [30,31]. NiO, which has a band gap of 3.45–4.0 eV, when exposed to visible and UV light, forms electron-hole pairs that can subsequently migrate to the catalyst's surface to aid in the breakdown of impurities. However, NiO's higher band gap and rapid recombination of e− and h+ pairs can limit its efficiency [32–34].

Our study introduces an innovative N-CQDs@NiO nanocomposite synthesized via a green, sustainable approach to address these challenges. Green synthesis of nanomaterials is still considered an emerging field. The traditional approach towards synthesizing photocatalysts includes chemical methods associated with hazardous chemicals, constituting an environmental footprint and fuel-less safe diagnostics. Due to the doping of N-CQDs into NiO, the electronic structure of NiO is profoundly changed, and the band gap of NiO is cut down. At the same time, easy generation of electrons and holes charge carriers could be separated, considerably enhancing photocatalytic efficiency under visible irradiation [35,36]. Such improved performance is especially vital for applications in environmental remediation where efficient degradation of complex pollutants is required. The interaction of N-CQDs@NiO results in the enhancement of ROS production, such as the hydroxyl radical (⋅OH) and the superoxide radical (⋅O2 −), which are critical to the degradation process for persistent pollutants such as 3-AP and SO dyes. It is superior to the literature that is currently available since it reveals that this nanocomposite can degrade pollutants faster and more completely than the previously reported systems, thus reinforcing its potential as an advanced material for wastewater treatment [37,38].

This work is unique because the authors synthesized excellent photocatalytic activity green N-CQDs@NiO nanocomposite. All previous studies of N-CQDs and NiO showed high pollutant degradation potential. However, in this new composite, only the light absorption and electron transport were improved, and the adverse impact on the environment was eliminated due to the synergistic properties of such elements. The optimal band gap tuning and visible light absorption make nanocomposite a better photocatalytic material than conventional materials. Therefore, this research introduces a new, environmentally friendly cleaning method beyond conventional photocatalysts. It has used this advancement to introduce new capabilities to this photocatalytic degradation technology of hazardous chemicals, like 3-AP and SO dyes.

Fresh Citrus limetta Fruit collected directly from local Fruit seller. Nickel nitrate pentahydrate, Urea, sodium hydroxide, Safranin-O dye, 3-Amino phenol, were purchased from Loba Chemie, India. Deionized water was used as the solvent for all the experimental methods. Methanol with a minimum purity of 99.9 %, acetonitrile (ACN) with a minimum purity of 99.9 %, and dichloromethane (DCM) with a minimum purity of 99.9 % were obtained from Sigma Aldrich. These chemicals are of HPLC quality.

Citrus limetta peels, initially cleaned and oven-dried at 50 °C, were pulverized into a fine powder. 5 g powdered peels was mixed with 40 ml distilled water in a beaker. Concurrently, a urea solution (2 g urea in 10 ml water) was prepared and introduced into the beaker. The mixture underwent microwave irradiation (800 W) for 12 min. Subsequently, distilled water was incorporated, and the resulting solution was subjected to centrifugation, filtration, and dialysis against distilled water for 48 h. The resultant N-CQDs solution was stored at 4 °C in the refrigerator (Scheme 1 a).

A 0.1 M aqueous nickel nitrate solution (100 mL) was agitated with 2 mL of green tea extract. Sodium hydroxide (0.1 M, 100 mL) was incrementally added until a basic pH (∼9) was attained, continuously stirring for approximately 30 min. The resulting dark brown solids were thoroughly washed with water and subjected to a pre-heated muffle furnace at 250 ± 10 °C for 4 h. Subsequently, dark brown-colored NiO nanoparticles were generated and stored in an airtight container for subsequent analysis.

The synthesis of the N-CQDs@NiO nanocomposite involved the addition of 2 mL of N-CQDs solution to a beaker containing a solution of Ni(NO3)2··6H2O (0.1 M, 100 mL), and 2 mL of plant extract. Sodium hydroxide (0.1 M, 100 mL) was gradually introduced until a basic pH (∼9) was achieved, with continuous stirring maintained for approximately 30 min. The precipitate underwent multiple washes with Millipore water, followed by filtration and drying at 70 °C. Subsequently, the powder was calcinated for 4 h at 250 ± 10 °C in a muffle furnace, producing dark brown N-CQDs@NiO nanoparticles. These nanoparticles were stored in a desiccator for further applications (Scheme 1b).

The produced catalysts were assessed for their photocatalytic activity by measuring the degradation of 3-AP and SO in an aqueous solution. The optimal levels of harmful pollutants 3-AP (10–50 mg/L) and SO (2–10 ppm), photocatalytic dose (5–20 mg), pH (3–11), and time were determined to produce the highest possible breakdown. Photocatalytic experiments were conducted using sun irradiation. Citrate buffers and borax were utilized to regulate the pH of the solutions during the experiment conducted in this investigation. The reaction mixtures were also evaluated under dark conditions to determine the influence of different radiation sources. Additionally, blank samples were analyzed to investigate the removal efficiency in-depth. Eq. (1) was employed to calculate the percentage degradation efficiency of photocatalysts, where Ci represents the initial concentration of pollutants, and Cf represents the final concentration.

The N-CQDs have a roughly spherical morphology and are uniformly distributed, according to the TEM picture (Fig. 1 a). These N-CQDs have a somewhat limited size distribution with an average diameter of roughly 3–5 nm. The size of this tiny object aligns with the anticipated measurements for quantum dots, which are recognized to display quantum confinement characteristics. The successful doping of nitrogen into the carbon lattice is confirmed by the HRTEM image and with XRD analysis, which also shows that the N-CQDs have a semi-crystalline structure with visible lattice fringes that match the (002) planes of graphitic carbon [39].

ce:section.ce:section-title: FE-SEM analysis NiO and N-CQDs@NiO nanocomposite
ce:section.ce:para: The FESEM images explain the morphological characteristics of NiO nanoparticles (Fig. 1b–c). The photos depict NiO as clumped particles with a slightly uneven form. These particles, which range in size from 20 to 50 nm, cluster together due to their high surface energy. NiO's rough and porous surface makes it helpful for catalytic applications by providing reactants with a larger surface area with which to interact. The high degree of crystallinity that the NiO nanoparticles appear to have, as evidenced by the FESEM images, is critical for maintaining their stability and reactivity throughout the photocatalytic activity. The N-CQDs@NiO nanocomposite was investigated to better understand the interaction between the two nanoparticles. The FE-SEM investigations reveal that the N-CQDs are evenly distributed on the surface of the NiO nanoparticles, forming a composite structure with a homogeneous coating that enhances overall stability and prevents nanocomposite aggregation. The N-CQDs@NiO nanocomposite structure likely enhances charge separation and transfer for photocatalytic applications, with the combined wide surface area of N-CQDs and the strong catalytic properties of NiO resulting in superior photocatalytic efficiency compared to the individual components [40].

The elemental compositions of N-CQDs, NiO, and the N-CQDs@NiO nanocomposite were determined by EDS analysis. The EDS spectra of N-CQDs confirmed the presence of carbon (C), nitrogen (N), and oxygen (O) molecules. The quantitative test revealed the following elemental composition: carbon (72.3 wt%), nitrogen (16.5 wt%), and oxygen (11.2 wt%). A noticeable nitrogen peak indicates that nitrogen has been successfully doped into the carbon matrix, which is required to modify the electrical properties of the carbon dots (Fig. 1g). NiO: The EDS analysis of NiO nanoparticles revealed that oxygen (O) and nickel (Ni) were the main peaks. The elemental composition of nickel oxide was discovered to be 78.4 wt% nickel and 21.6 wt% oxygen based on the expected stoichiometry (Fig. 1h). NiO@N-CQD The EDS spectra of the N-CQDs@NiO nanocomposite showed peaks for carbon (C), nitrogen (N), oxygen (O), and nickel (Ni), indicating that N-CQDs were successfully integrated with NiO. The elemental composition was found using quantitative analysis: N = 7.5 wt%, O = 24.1 wt%, Ni = 39.7 wt%, and C = 28.7 wt%. The carbon and nitrogen peaks in the composite indicate that N-CQDs are uniformly distributed throughout the NiO matrix (Fig. 1i). The homogeneous distribution of these elements demonstrates that the synthesis procedure resulted in a well-mixed nanocomposite.

PXRD patterns reveal N-CQDs, NiO, and N-CQDs@NiO nanocomposite crystallographic structures. N-CQDs show an XRD pattern with a prominent peak centered at 2θ = 25°, similar to amorphous carbon materials (Fig. 2 a). This broad peak represents graphitic carbon structures within carbon dots. The NiO XRD pattern shows distinct peaks at 2θ values 37.2°, 43.3°, 62.9°, 75.4°, and 79.4°. According to JCPDS card no. 47-1049, these peaks correspond to the cubic NiO planes (110), (200), (220), (310), and (222). These different peaks confirm the crystalline structure of the generated NiO nanoparticles (Fig. 2b).

The XRD pattern of the N-CQDs@NiO nanocomposite shows peaks for both N-CQDs and NiO. The composite contains amorphous N-CQDs, as indicated by a prominent peak at 2θ = 25°. Fig. 2c shows distinct peaks at 34.2°, 39.2°, 59.3°, and 75.4°, indicating the presence of crystalline NiO in the composite. The XRD investigation results demonstrate the successful synthesis of N-CQDs@NiO nanocomposite containing crystalline NiO and amorphous carbon dots.

Fig. 2a, the N-CQDs exhibit a broad peak near 3300–3400 cm−1 in the FTIR spectrum. This peak is due to NH and OH stretching vibrations, signifying the presence of amino and hydroxyl groups on the surface. Another significant peak at 1550 cm−1 corresponds to CC or CO stretching, suggesting the existence of sp2 hybridized carbon and potentially carbonyl groups. A noticeable peak at 1460 cm−1 relates to CH bending vibrations. Additionally, a peak at 1067 cm−1 is attributed to CO stretching vibrations, indicating the presence of oxygenated functional groups on the N-CQDs.

Two firm peaks can be seen in the NiO FTIR spectra at 3485 cm−1 and 1575 cm−1. OH stretching vibrations show the presence of hydroxyl groups at a peak of 3485 cm−1. NiO stretching vibrations are represented by the peak at 625 cm−1, which verifies the synthesis of NiO nps (Fig. 2e). N-CQDs and NiO exhibit distinctive peaks in the N-CQDs@NiO nanocomposite's FTIR spectra. The broad peak around 3480 cm−1 can be ascribed to the overlapping OH stretching vibrations from N-CQDs and NiO. CO/CC and CO from N-CQDs are indicated by peaks at 1620 cm−3 and 1370 cm−3, respectively (Fig. 2f). The presence of NiO from NiO is confirmed by the peak at 645 cm−1, showing that the composite has successfully formed. These findings from the FTIR study demonstrate that N-CQDs@NiO nanocomposite were successfully synthesized.

The XPS spectra of the N-CQDs@NiO nanocomposite reveal essential information about its surface elemental composition and chemical states. The survey spectrum shows signals for carbon (C 1s), nitrogen (N 1s), oxygen (O 1s), and nickel (Ni 2p), indicating that N-CQDs were successfully incorporated into the NiO matrix (Fig. 3 a). Ni2P Spectrum: The high-resolution Ni 2p spectra reveal the nickel oxidation status in the nanocomposites. The satellite peaks at 861.1 and 879.3 eV correspond to the Ni 2p3/3 and Ni 2p1/3 peaks at 854.8 and 872.6 eV, respectively. These binding energies, typical of Ni3 in NiO, indicate the composite's NiO content. The satellite peaks of NiO nanoparticles indicate that Ni3+ is in a high-spin state (Fig. 3b). N-1 spectrum. The nitrogen doping in the carbon dots is seen in the high-resolution N 1s spectrum. The pyrrolic N (N-5) spectra peak is 399.6 eV. Graphitic N (N-Q) is responsible for another peak at 401.2 eV, indicating that nitrogen has been successfully incorporated into the carbon framework. These nitrogen species increase the N-CQDs' ability to donate electrons, making charge separation in the N-CQDs@NiO nanocomposite more efficient. C 1s Spectrum: The N-CQDs@NiO nanocomposite's high-resolution C 1s spectrum shows peaks from numerous carbon species. The N-CQDs' graphitic structure suggests sp2-hybridized carbon (CC) is responsible for the most prominent peak at 284.6 eV. At 285.5 eV, a secondary signal indicates sp3-hybridized carbon (CC/CH). Furthermore, the CN and CO groups are responsible for the 286.6 and 288.8 eV peaks, respectively. These peaks indicate the carboxyl groups and nitrogen doping present in the N-CQDs, which are required to improve the composite's photocatalytic properties (Fig. 3d). O-1 Spectrum: A peak in the O 1s spectrum at 530.2 eV corresponds to the metal–oxygen bonds (NiO) seen in the NiO structure. Another signal at 531.8 eV is caused by CO bonds formed by carboxyl groups in N-CQDs (Fig. 3e). The presence of these oxygen species confirms that the composite was successfully formed and that N-CQDs and NiO interacted. The high-resolution spectra show that the N-CQDs contain carboxyl groups, nitrogen doping, sp2, and sp2 hybridized carbon, and Ni2+ in the NiO structure. These findings demonstrate that N-CQDs and NiO may be successfully synthesised and combined to form a nanocomposite with enhanced photocatalytic properties.

Fig. 4 a displays the FL emission spectra of N-CQDs when excited with wavelengths between 190 and 260 nm. The peak intensities of FL emission of N-CQDs exhibit wavelength dependence, which can be attributed to the distinct functional groups on the N-CQDs. These groups are excitation energy traps, resulting in defects [41,42]. The surface properties of N-CQDs, including their functional group composition, are associated with variants in FL emission. Additionally, surface modifications, particle size, and heteroatoms play a role [43–45]. When excited at 240 nm, the emission spectra exhibit a maximum intensity at 776 nm. The quantum yield (Fig. S1) was calculated to be 38.48 % using quinine sulfate as a reference (54 %) to further assess the fluorescence properties [46,47].

The FL spectra illustrated (Fig. S2) a comparison of the optical activity between pure NiO nanoparticles and the N-CQDs@NiO nanocomposite. Both samples exhibit a pronounced emission peak around 550 nm, signifying intrinsic defects and oxygen vacancies in NiO. The emission intensity is the most significant difference between the two spectra, with the N-CQDs@NiO nanocomposite showing a substantially higher FL intensity than pure NiO. This enhanced intensity indicates that N-CQDs improve charge separation efficiency and reduce non-radiative electron-hole recombination. The efficient transfer of photoexcited electrons from N-CQDs to the conduction band of NiO boosts radiative recombination, enhancing overall optical performance.

N-CQDs also introduce surface functional groups and defect states, broadening the emission spectrum and increasing the FL intensity. NiO and N-CQDs@NiO samples exhibit broad emission between 300 and 450 nm, suggesting the presence of various defect and surface states. In this region, the N-CQDs@NiO composite consistently outperforms pure NiO, corroborating the notion that N-CQDs suppress electron-hole recombination and improve photoluminescence. The results show that the N-CQDs@NiO nanocomposite demonstrates superior FL compared to pure NiO. Incorporating N-CQDs inhibits non-radiative recombination pathways, enhancing radiative charge carrier recombination. This makes the nanocomposite a promising material for optoelectronic and photocatalytic applications, as the enhancement in FL intensity reflects improved charge separation and potential photocatalytic efficiency.

Characterizing band gap properties in NPs involves employing UV–visible reflectance analysis. Utilizing the Tauc equation, which depends on the absorption coefficient (α) and energy represented as ((αhѵ)2 = A(hѵ − Eg)), is a common approach to determining the band gap of nanomaterials [48]. By plotting (αhν)2 against energy, the optical band gaps of NiO and N-CQDs@NiO were 3.12 eV and 2.87 eV, respectively (Fig. 4b-–c–). The observed decrease in the band gap energy of N-CQDs@NiO implies heightened absorption of visible light and the possibility of better photocatalytic activity when exposed to solar radiation. The band gap has been modified due to the insertion of N-CQDs, which improve light absorption and charge carrier dynamics by introducing extra electronic states into the NiO band structure.

Zeta Potential analysis is a technique used to evaluate the stability and surface charge of colloidal NPs. The Zeta Potential study indicated that the NiO nanomaterial had a value of −9.73 mV, while the N-CQDs@NiO nanocomposite had a value of 32.7 mV (Fig. 4d-–e–). These results show that the nanocomposite has a more stable structure and is less likely to experience flocculation or coagulation. Similar results have been reported for ZnO [49] and CQDs@ZnO NPs [50].

The BET analysis of N-CQDs@NiO nanocomposites reveals notable structural modifications compared to pristine NiO. The nitrogen adsorption–desorption isotherm exhibits a type IV isotherm with a clear hysteresis loop, characteristic of mesoporous materials (Fig. S3a). The specific surface area of N-CQDs@NiO is 52.3 m2/g, which is lower than the 88.33 m2/g of pure NiO (Vipin et al., 2024). This reduction is likely due to incorporating N-CQDs, which may block surface pores, decreasing the available surface area. Furthermore, the pore size distribution shows an average peak at 4.93 nm, indicating a greater prevalence of mesopores in the nanocomposite (Fig. S3b). In comparison, pure NiO exhibits a larger average pore size of 28 nm [51]. This shift towards smaller mesopores suggests that including N-CQDs modifies the pore structure, improving the adsorption efficiency for smaller molecules. A similar trend is observed in other composite systems, such as CuFe2O4@CQD, where surface area and pore size decrease with quantum dot integration, yet enhanced electronic properties lead to superior overall performance [52].

Despite the reduced surface area, the N-CQDs@NiO nanocomposites exhibit excellent photocatalytic activity. Adding N-CQDs improves the electronic properties by enhancing charge separation and transfer, compensating for the lower surface area. The mesoporous structure, with average pores in the 4–5 nm range, facilitates effective diffusion and adsorption of pollutants, ensuring the material's high photocatalytic efficiency. This balance between decreased surface area and enhanced electronic properties makes N-CQDs@NiO a highly promising material for environmental remediation. Similar performance enhancements are reported in CuFe2O4@CQD composites and NiO@CQDs [53], where quantum dot incorporation improves electronic characteristics despite reductions in surface area and pore size [52,53].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Impact of pH, concentration and catalyic dose
ce:section.ce:para: The primary objective of these investigations is to evaluate the photocatalytic application of the NiO and N-CQDs@NiO nanocomposites for the remediation of environmental pollutants (3-AP and SO) under sunlight. For the degradation of 3-AP and SO, various factors such as pollutant concentration (SO = 2–10 mg L−1, 3-AP = 10–50 mg L−1), catalyst amount (5–25 mg), solution pH (3–11), time (SO = 0–2 h, 3-AP = 0–5 h), and type of irradiation source (dark and sunlight) were optimized (Fig 5. a-f). The optimal degradation of SO and 3-AP occurred at initial concentrations of 2 mg/L and 10 mg/L, respectively, utilizing 15 mg of catalyst at a neutral pH under sunlight exposure. Under optimized conditions, N-CQDs@NiO demonstrated superior removal efficiency (SO: 98 %; 3-AP: 99 %) compared to the parent nanoparticle NiO (SO: 45 %; 3-AP: 60 %), which can be attributed to its ability of high adsorption, doping mediated enhanced stability, higher adsorption efficiency, and generation of significant quantities of hydroxyl or singlet oxygen with reduced aggregation tendencies. It was observed that, as the catalytic dose increases from 5 to 15 mg, the % removal of RB and NP increases, reaching a maximum at 15 mg and then decreasing. The initial increase in catalytic dose provides more active sites for the degradation process, enhancing the removal efficiency. However, beyond a certain point (15 mg), the excess catalyst may limit the availability of active sites, leading to a decrease in efficiency [54]. In concentration optimization, it was observed that the most significant removal was observed at the lowest concentration (i.e., 2 mg L−1 for SO and 10 mg L−1 for 3-AP). This could be because more pollutant molecules compete for the same number of active sites on the catalyst at higher concentrations, lowering the overall removal efficiency [55]. SO and 3-AP exhibit maximum degradation at neutral pH because the catalyst surface may have a balanced charge distribution at neutral pH, reducing repulsion between similarly charged species and enhancing the adsorption of pollutants. Furthermore, a pH of neutrality often provides the best conditions for several chemical interactions, such as hydrogen bonding, van der Waals forces, and π-π interactions. These interactions can be crucial to the adsorption and removal process and assist in clarifying the maximum removal efficiency observed [56].

Under optimized pollutant concentration, catalytic dose, and pH, the degradation of SO and 3-AP was also studied under dark, artificial bulb, and sunlight conditions. The removal of both pollutants (SO and 3-AP) by photocatalyst was found to be higher in natural sunlight (98–99 %), followed by bulbs (35–37 %) and dark (12–15 %) (Fig. 7a). The higher degradation in light could result from enough availability of energy to excite electrons from the valence band (VB) to the conduction band (CB). Upon excitation, electron-hole pairs generate active species that break down the large structure of SO and 3-AP into minor by-products.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Impact of temperature and oxygen availability
ce:section.ce:para: A series of controlled experiments were conducted to evaluate the influence of temperature and dissolved oxygen levels on the photocatalytic efficiency of the synthesized N-CQDs@NiO nanocomposites. For the temperature influence study, aqueous solutions of 2 mg L−1 for SO and 10 mg L−1 for 3-AP were prepared along with 15 mg of N-CQDs@NiO nanocomposites used. The photocatalytic degradation experiments were carried out at various controlled temperatures, namely 15 °C, 20 °C, 25 °C, 30 °C and 35 °C utilizing a temperature-controlled water bath to ensure precision in temperature maintenance. Degradation efficiency was monitored at regular time intervals using UV–Vis spectroscopy to assess the impact of varying temperatures on degradation kinetics (Fig. S4a). In parallel, the effect of dissolved oxygen levels was investigated by preparing identical photocatalytic degradation setups. Dissolved oxygen concentrations were varied by bubbling different gases through the solutions before and during the photocatalytic reaction. Air bubbling was employed to provide standard dissolved oxygen levels, oxygen bubbling was used to enhance dissolved oxygen concentration, and nitrogen bubbling created a low-oxygen environment. The degradation rates under these varying oxygen conditions were also measured using UV–Vis spectroscopy (Fig. S4b). This comprehensive approach allowed for a detailed assessment of how temperature and dissolved oxygen availability influence the photocatalytic efficiency of N-CQDs@NiO nanocomposites in aquatic environments. Under optimized temperature and dissolved oxygen levels. The removal of both pollutants (SO and 3-AP) by photocatalyst was found to be higher in 35 °C temperature (98–99 %), followed by nitrogen and oxygen environment (18–19 %) and dark (100 %).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic performance of N-CQDs@NiO in wastewater samples
ce:section.ce:para: The application of the developed N-CQDs@NiO photocatalyst in real wastewater samples is a critical step in validating the effectiveness and practicality of the designed method. In this study, we spiked real wastewater samples with 2 mg L−1 of SO and 10 mg L−1 of 3-AP, and 15 mg of N-CQDs@NiO. Under laboratory conditions, SO was wholly degraded in 120 min, while 3-AP took 5 h (300 min). However, in the real wastewater sample, the degradation times were extended due to the more complex matrix present in the sample (Fig. S5). Despite the longer degradation times observed in the real wastewater, N-CQDs@NiO exhibited significant photocatalytic activity, confirming its effectiveness for real-world applications. The more challenging degradation kinetics in the wastewater sample can be attributed to various factors, including other organic and inorganic pollutants, which could compete for active sites on the photocatalyst and reduce light penetration. While expected in real samples, these interferences did not prevent the successful degradation of both pollutants, highlighting the robustness of the designed N-CQDs@NiO catalyst. The performance of N-CQDs@NiO in real wastewater conditions reinforces the efficiency of the photocatalyst, even in non-ideal environments. This indicates that our product is effective under controlled laboratory conditions and shows promise in real sample applications. The slight increase in degradation time reflects the inherent complexity of real wastewater, yet the catalyst maintained its capability to degrade both SO and 3-AP efficiently.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Removal efficiency of N-CQDs@NiO among various organic pollutants
ce:section.ce:para: The bar chart in Fig. S4c illustrates the percentage of different organic pollutants removed using the catalyst. The contaminants tested include chlorpyrifos (CP), chrysene, fluorene (representing polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons), bisphenol A (BPA), and nonylphenol (NP). The results indicate high removal efficiency across all tested pollutants, each showing above 80 % removal. The catalyst achieved approximately 85 % removal of chlorophenol (CP), demonstrating its ability to degrade pesticides known for their toxicity and environmental persistence. For PAHs, represented by chrysene and fluorene, the removal efficiency was close to 90 %, underscoring the catalyst's effectiveness in breaking down complex, hydrophobic molecules that are typically resistant to degradation due to their stable aromatic structures. In the case of bisphenol A (BPA), a well-known endocrine disruptor, the catalyst achieved nearly 95 % removal, indicating its potential for addressing pollutants that interfere with hormonal systems. Similarly, nonylphenol (NP), another persistent organic pollutant with endocrine-disrupting effects, was removed with about 90 % efficiency. These findings demonstrate the broad-spectrum applicability of the catalyst, which effectively degrades various organic pollutants from different chemical classes. The consistently high removal rates across various molecular structures, including aromatic compounds, halogenated phenols, and endocrine disruptors, reinforce the catalyst's versatility and potential in environmental cleanup and water treatment applications.

Under the most efficient conditions, the N-CQDs@NiO nanocomposite achieved a degradation rate of 98 % for SO and 99 % for 3-AP, surpassing the degradation capabilities of the individual NPs. The enhanced degradation efficiency of the N-CQDs@NiO nanocomposite can be attributed to its stability, capacity to generate substantial amounts of hydroxyl or free radicals, and reduced particle aggregation. The enhanced charge separation and active radical generation mechanisms observed in this study are similar to those reported in co-catalytic systems, such as Fe/Mo cycles and Fenton-like reactions, where accelerated electron transfer and high-valent radical formation significantly improve photocatalytic performance [57,58]. The addition of N-CQDs to NiO plays a critical role in stabilizing the photocatalyst through two main mechanisms: (1) N-CQDs provide a greater surface area, promoting adsorption and rapid transfer of pollutants to active sites; (2) N-CQDs improve charge separation by extending the lifetime of photogenerated electrons and holes, thus reducing the recombination rate, which is a significant limitation in conventional photocatalysis. The process of eliminating pollutants consists of two steps: firstly, pollutants are adsorbed onto the surfaces of nanocatalysts, and secondly, they are destroyed through photocatalysis. This removal is supported by the semiconducting properties of the nanocatalysts, resulting in the production of oxidative species [59,60]. Adsorption typically consists of processes such as the transfer of mass, resistance within the medium, dispersion of particles, and adhesion to the surface of a catalyst. Diffusion resistance plays a significant role in the mass transfer process, namely in the adsorption of pollutants on active sites of nanocatalysts. However, the photocatalytic removal process generates active molecules, such as hydroxyl radicals and free radicals, through light absorption, initial adsorption, and charge transfer [60]. N-CQDs@NiO nanocomposite enhances the adsorption rate, promoting the fast arrival of SO and 3-AP molecules at the active sites. The photocatalyst, covered in pollutants, was subjected to sunshine, resulting in the movement of electrons from the VB to the CB. When a photon from sunlight is absorbed, it generates “h+” (positively charged holes) in the VB [61,62]. The hybrid link plays a vital role in controlling the longevity of electrons and holes. This impacts the movement of electric charges in the VB and CB, respectively [rani 55] (Fig. 6 a). The duration of the presence of charged active species substantially impacts the photocatalytic removal process. Adding N-CQDs to NiO results in an extended period of charge separation, preventing the recombination of holes (h+) and electrons (e−). Highly reactive electrons undergo reactions with dissolved oxygen, forming superoxide anions (O2 −). They also react with water, leading to the production of hydroxyl radicals. Similarly, holes react with water to generate hydroxyl radicals. Hydroperoxyl radicals are generated by the interaction between O2 molecules and h+ [63]. In addition to HOO radicals, the superoxide anion can also generate OH radicals [64]. The subsequent equations can be employed to delineate the overarching mechanism responsible for the generation and subsequent decay of active radicals:

The LC-MS investigation utilized mass spectra and total ion chromatogram (TIC) data to outline potential pathways for the degradation of SO and 3-AP pollutants (Fig. S7). These analyses characterized the degradation of SO and 3-AP, highlighting the generation of active species from sunlight that produce hydroxyl radicals essential for pollutant removal. The primary objective of the LC-MS analysis was to identify degraded metabolites and side products of SO and 3-AP. Results showed distinct peaks corresponding to these metabolites, indicating the breakdown of pollutants into less harmful byproducts such as water (H2O) and carbon dioxide (CO2). Fig. 6b. delineates these degradation pathways, emphasizing the transformation of pollutants into safer forms. The degradation of Safranine O (SO) dye was investigated using a N-CQDs@NiO nanocomposite, as depicted in the provided mechanism. Safranine O, a triphenylmethane dye, initially shows a molecular ion peak at m/z = 350, confirming its intact form. Its structure features two amino groups on phenyl rings and a central nitrogen atom, essential for its color properties. With the catalyst, SO undergoes chemical changes, forming an intermediate 3,7-dimethylphenazine-2,8-diamine with a peak at m/z = 238.12, indicating cleavage of a phenyl ring and partial loss of conjugation. This intermediate retains the amino groups, reflecting early degradation stages. Further breakdown yields a simpler molecule 5-methylbenzene-1,2,4-triol with a peak at m/z = 140, identified as benzenetriol (hydroxyquinol), signifying complete degradation into a more oxidized form. The process involves hydrolysis and oxidation, demonstrating the catalyst’s effectiveness in transforming complex dyes into less harmful, simpler compounds and highlighting its potential for treating dye-contaminated wastewater.

Similarly, 3-aminophenol (3-AP) degradation was examined, as outlined in the mechanism (Fig. 6b). 3-aminophenol, an aromatic compound, initially presents a molecular ion peak at m/z = 109.05, confirming its intact form. It features an amino and hydroxyl group on a benzene ring, crucial for its reactivity. When introducing hydroxyl radicals (OH·), 3-AP undergoes several transformations. The first intermediate, with a peak at m/z = 93.06, indicates partial degradation, possibly forming a biphenyl derivative. This intermediate further degrades into phenol (m/z = 94.04) and aniline (m/z = 93.03), reflecting ring cleavage and loss of functional groups. Further degradation yields smaller fragments like ethanolamine (m/z = 73.05), glyoxal (m/z = 86.04), water, and carbon dioxide, highlighting hydrolytic and oxidative pathways. The process demonstrates the catalyst's efficiency in breaking down complex aromatic compounds into simpler, environmentally benign molecules, showcasing its potential for treating contaminated environments [65].

The photodegradation kinetics of SO (2 mg L−1) and 3-AP (10 mg L−1) were investigated with time under optimized conditions (pH ∼ 7.0; catalyst loading: 15 mg). The removal rate initially experienced a quick decline, followed by a slow drop, consistent with first-order kinetics (Fig. 7 b-c). This phenomenon results from the semiconducting nature that causes energized electrons to have an increased lifespan when exposed to sunshine. Sunshine exposure leads to large quantities of free radicals, quickly attacking the pollutant molecules via oxidation or hydroxylation [66]. The nanocomposite (N-CQDs@NiO) demonstrated exceptional pollutant degradation capability, with maximum removal percentages of 98 % for SO and 99 % for 3-AP within 120 min and 5 h of sunshine exposure to SO and 3-AP, respectively. Table 2 displays the k-values and half-lives for the composite and original nanomaterials. The nanocomposite (N-CQDs@NiO) exhibited a higher k value and a shorter t1/2, suggesting that the targeted pollutants were degraded faster than the native NiO NPs.

Langmuir and Freundlich models were used to study the adsorption isotherm for the degradation of SO and 3-AP (Fig. S6a–b). The Langmuir isotherm showed the most significant and favorable findings among the tested models. It had a high correlation coefficient (R2, 0.991 for SO and 0.994 for 3-AP) and a very low p-value (4.441 × 10−4 for SO and 1.299 × 10−4 for 3-AP) for the adsorption of harmful pollutants SO and 3-AP, as illustrated in Table S1. The Langmuir-adsorption isotherm formulation was used to calculate the maximum adsorption capacity (Xm) for NiO (0.6398 mg g−1), N-CQDs@NiO (0.9237 mg g−1) for SO, as well as NiO (0.377 mg g−1), N-CQDs@NiO (0.73546 mg g−1) for 3-AP. Therefore, N-CQDs@NiO is more effective in its maximum adsorption capacity, making it more suitable for the degradation and adsorption of both SO and 3-AP.

The photocatalytic reaction mechanism of the SO and 3-AP were determined by scavenging experiment with a) without scavenger b) 2 mL tert-butyl alcohol (t-BuOH) c) 2 mL para-benzoquinone (pBZQ) d) ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid disodium salt (Na2EDTA) and 10 mL of Pollutant solution under optimized conditions. Na2EDTA is used to scavenge holes [67,68], t-BuOH is used to scavenge OH radicals [69], pBZQ is used as ·O2 − scavenger [70]. The degradation efficiency declined from 99 % to 14 % for 3-AP and from 98 % to 17 % for SO with t-BuOH, pBZQ, and Na2EDTA, respectively (Fig. 7d). It was observed that the degradation of SO and 3-AP significantly decreased in the case of the t-BuOH scavenger, suggesting that OH radicals were the main species responsible for the RB and NP removal. However, there is a slight decrease in removal efficiency with pBZQ and Na2EDTA, suggesting that these species have only a minor role in the degradation.

The efficacy of synthesized N-CQDs@NiO nanocomposite in terms of recyclability was explored in the study to eliminate pollutants when exposed to the sun naturally. Two elements dictate the photochemical durability of a photocatalyst: its capacity to effortlessly segregate and its resistance to inactivity. Following each run, the catalyst was centrifuged to remove the photocatalyst from the sample, cleaned many times using distilled water and acetone, and then heated to 60 °C for six hours to assess its recyclability. This rejuvenated catalyst was utilized for a subsequent 3AP and SO decomposition round. The nanocomposite demonstrated remarkable durability by being used six times in a row under identical removal conditions while consistently maintaining its photocatalytic effectiveness and generating reactive species. The slight decrease in the efficiency of pollutant elimination observed in every successive round could be linked to the accumulation of pollutants on the surface of the catalyst (Fig. 7e). After undergoing six cycles, pollutants might accumulate on the surface of a mixture of NPs, blocking the active sites responsible for absorbing photons and leading to a decrease in the degradation process. The PXRD pattern of N-CQDs@NiO nanocomposite following the Ist, 3rd, and 6th cycle confirmed no alterations in the composition of the nanocomposites (Fig. 7f).

The project concluded with effectively synthesizing nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) from citrus limetta peels, N-CQDs@NiO nanocomposite, and NiO nanoparticles utilizing green tea extract. Comprehensive evaluation using FTIR, PXRD, XPS, and Zeta potential analysis verified that N-CQDs were successfully incorporated into the NiO matrix, preserving the nanocomposite's structural integrity and improving its photocatalytic capabilities. When exposed to sunlight, the N-CQDs@NiO nanocomposite showed a higher degradation efficiency for 3-AP and SO pollutants than NiO nanoparticles alone, according to photocatalytic tests. Maximum removal effectiveness of 99 % for 3-AP and 98 % for SO was achieved under ideal degradation circumstances caused by the N-CQDs' improved adsorption capacity, decreased particle aggregation, and efficient charge separation. Additionally, the study clarified the mechanism of photocatalytic degradation, emphasizing the part played by reactive species, including superoxide anions and hydroxyl radicals, in the pollutant degradation process. The N-CQDs@NiO nanocomposite had a greater adsorption capacity, according to adsorption isotherm models and kinetic investigations that validated first-order reaction kinetics. The N-CQDs@NiO nanocomposite exhibits excellent promise for environmental remediation applications, offering an effective and long-lasting remedy for the breakdown of organic contaminants in wastewater. Subsequent investigations may enhance the synthesis procedure and investigate the feasible integration of these nanocomposites in actual wastewater treatment systems.
